sentences.text: It was January 1943.
My mother just died.
And I was talking to you about what we did during the day in Birkenau.
The block I-- the camp I was in, the B2B, were only Czech families who came from Theresienstadt-- the transport in September and our transport in December.
We would be sitting on our beds, and we would cook-- how we would do this and how we would do that.
And it was all imaginary.
How we would take a bath.
How wonderful it would be, that warm water sitting in the bathtub.
And you know, till this day, every day when I take a bath, I'm back there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to go out twice a day to be counted.
So this was our main chore in a way.
And it was a chore, because we stood there being counted on and on.
It was winter.
We had hardly anything on.
We had no shoes.
We had these pantoufles , which probably was good.
We had no frozen toes, not much of a coat.
And we would be standing there till they counted everybody.
Sometimes the count didn't tally, because, you see, the dead people were in the back of the barracks, and they also had to go and count them.
And sometimes the SS men forgot to count them.
So when they tallied the whole thing up, there were some people missing.
So if they didn't have the right count, they started all over again.
So we would sometimes stand there for hours.
And we did that in the morning, and we did that at night-- I mean, in the afternoon.
So this was our work.
Sometimes we went out of camp and-- and collected stones.
Why stones?
Because the men would make the buildings-- the road of the camp.
So we provided them with the stones.
But that wasn't really that much.
Later on, some young women were making the band for-- for the rifles.
How would you call that?
No, no.
The-- the--
The strap.
And it was made mostly out of jute, and that was with tar.
And their skin, their pores, were are all blocked with the tar.
So they really were a mess.
But they got cigarettes for that, and that's where we all started to smoke.
It was kind of good.
It was good for the hunger.
And that's where I had my first cigarette, in Birkenau.
Funny place to start to smoke.
Oh, I think I didn't show you my number.
And here you can see where they tattooed me.
I have it quite low.
Most of the people, as I said before, have it further up.
But I couldn't move it up, and so it's here.
When you asked me before what my number was, I somehow, at the moment, I forgot, because it is really part of me.
It's like somebody has a birthmark.
They don't see it.
I had-- I went to-- to a doctor.
Yeah, I needed some small surgery.
And he saw it and he said, Mrs.
Ivry, would you like that I should take it out?
I said no.
I know it's there, but I want other people should see it and should be reminded.
So I will never take it off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
It-- it was the beginning of March '44.
And there was a law in Auschwitz that prisoners were allowed to live six months.
We signed that, and also the previous transport signed it.
Because they saw that nobody's going to live six months, and the ones who survive, they'll fix it.
So the transport where my mother and brother came was on the 15th of September, and here was the beginning of March.
What happened was they took all the people who came on the 15th of September, and they put them in our camp next to us.
It was B2A where they assembled them and on March 8 killed them during the night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We heard them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put them on trucks.
The night-- the day before I saw my brother, and I had a pair of mittens.
And I said, would you like to have my mittens?
And he said, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] where I am going, you don't need mittens.
I'm sorry.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
We heard the trucks during the night.
They were singing [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Czech national anthem.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how they went to the gas chamber, all of them.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Among them was a young man by the name of Freddie Hirsch.
He used to be my gym teacher.
He came from Germany to-- to Prague, and he was a very fine young man.
He loved children.
He worked with them.
He took care of them.
And also, in-- in Birkenau, he was with the children.
And all the children went, and he went with them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was March 9, 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Half the camp was empty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were walking around like [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] half alive.
We knew that the same fate will be our fate.
But we had to continue, and we had to be strong because we couldn't show the Germans that we are going to just fade away.
We didn't know that the war was going very badly for them, for the invasion took place in the spring of '44.
And so they really had a problem on their hands.
And one day, they called us all out and said that we are going to-- that we have to go through selection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lined up.
We had to undress completely, carry our clothes on-- over our right arm.
And there was Dr.
Mengele with his henchmen, moving his thumb to the left and to the right.
And I was chosen to live.
We went twice or three times through these selections.
The names were marked.
And the ones who were chosen were all young girls and young men, and the rest of them stayed there.
We had an assistant to the elder of the camp.
His name was Willie.
And he would go to young women who had children, because there were children with us, and he said to them, if you want to live, you have to leave your children.
But nobody did.
At the beginning of July, they moved us out, the women, to the women's camp, which was just north of us, I would think.
Perhaps it was south.
It was-- the tracks were between our camp and the women's camp.
Because later on, they-- they built a train track right to the-- to the gas chamber.
It was more efficient.
So we came into-- we marched into the women's camp.
Talking of craziness-- there was a band playing.
I think that you saw the movie about Fenelon.
She was there conducting the band, and we were marching into this.
I don't remember what they were playing, but they were playing a marching song, and we  marched in.
The inmates of the women's camp didn't like us very much.
After all, we had hair.
And they knew that we are going to be moved out from Auschwitz.
Because still then, nobody left Auschwitz unless you escaped somehow, like one of our prisoners did with the help of one SS man, which I will relate to you now.
As I told you, we went out to pick the stones.
So they used to come with us, SS men, and also some prisoners, some men prisoners.
The SS man was by the name of Pistek.
He was a Romanian of German origin.
And as we were picking those stones to take them with us, I saw them pointing at different places and talking.
And I said to myself, there's something fishy going on.
Sure enough, the next day the sirens went, and the sirens meant that somebody escaped.
Well, this SS man took this prisoner and gave him a uniform, and they-- he had arranged with a farmer to give him a-- a bicycle.
And what did he do?
He went back to Theresienstadt, and he told people that they shouldn't go in the transport, what-- and told them what awaits them.
But they didn't believe him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So now I'm going back to [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't-- the prisoners, there were a lot of young Slovak-- I mean Jewish girls from Slovakia.
I-- you know, people don't know here that in Slovakia they had SS, but they were Slovaks.
And they were called Hlinka Guards, because the-- the man who was in charge, his name was Hlinka, and so they were called the Hlinka Guards.
In Slovakia, the Jews were rounded up by the Slovaks.
So now we-- we know that in Canada there's a few of them here, and they're going to be tried, hopefully.
And they said they never did anything, but they did.
They helped.
So these girls were there in-- in Auschwitz or in Birkenau since 1942.
And this was 1944, so they were very tough and, of course, had a very long time being there.
It was-- it was awful for them.
And seeing us with our hair and thinking that we are going to leave, they-- they weren't very kind to us.
So we also were-- they searched us before we left.
They searched everything, what we had, also internally.
A woman doctor would search us whether we have nothing hidden.
We were divided into different groups, and we were running back and forth.
We always wanted to be with our friends.
We were then chosen.
So many go there and so-- we didn't know where we were going.
And we were put on the train, and we saw that it was soldiers who were with us, not SS.
So we knew there must be something good for us.
Because if the SS is not coming with us, something must be going on.
And away we went on the train.
We traveled a day, a night, a day.
We didn't know where we were going.
German soldiers, old men who couldn't go to the front.
And we arrived in Hamburg.
It was, I believe, on my birthday, on the 7th of July.
And we were brought into Freihafen.
It-- warehouses.
On one side they were on the water, the other side on the street.
And they were in two big rooms with 500 girls, I believe.
And that's where we stayed, and we went to work.
The first night we experienced a very violent storm or whatever it was.
We didn't know what it was.
It was an air raid.
We were not going anywhere.
They didn't take-- let us go anywhere.
We stayed, and the air raid was going on.
And in the morning, we saw some places burning and things like that.
And we went to work.
They assigned us to different places.
I went to a factory where we were cleaning up things.
We were walking here, there.
There were air raids during the day.
There were air raids-- air raids during-- air raids during the night.
We were working with bricks.
We were moving them from one place to the other.
Most of the time, we were just standing there.
We didn't get much to eat.
We-- we got to eat where we worked, so that wasn't too bad.
What we lacked was sleep.
We were just like in a daze all the time, because we had to get up very early.
During the night, we had the air raids.
And so we lacked sleep.
We stayed there in Freihafen, I believe, till the beginning of the fall.
I'm not quite sure of the time, how long.
From there, they moved us to Neugraben.
That was a little camp.
We were just the 500 of us.
And we were doing all kinds of things.
We were brought in a-- in a sandpit, taking the sand and putting on little wagons.
And I was not working in the sandpit.
I was helping the engineer of the little train to change the track.
That was my job.
He was a communist.
And he had to go-- I don't recall exactly-- either it was once every day or only once a week to the police that he was still there.
And we had a foreman there.
He was-- he always brought us some-- some little things.
And-- oh, yes.
I made a-- I made a friend.
When I was in Freihafen, there were Italian prisoners of war also in the same block.
And whenever I went to work in the morning, there was this very handsome man, or young soldier.
He was like a Red Cross-- he had a red cross.
He was like a nurse or something.
And he would go like this, kind of a greeting.
And-- and so I would-- I would go like this, and this was our greeting.
And every day he would stand there and wait.
And one day-- we could look out in the back to-- and they would look out [? them. ?] So we made a date.
Crazy, right?
We made a date that we are going to meet in this cellar downstairs.
Sure enough, we met.
We couldn't speak.
I didn't speak Italian.
He didn't speak German or Czech.
It was all by hand.
So only he says that, mama, he has a mama.
And the war finished, I have to come.
And his name was Lidio Grassano.
This same Lidio Grassano came to see me when I worked in Neugraben, and he brought me some food.
He really took a chance.
He had to take a train and see me.
As a matter of fact, not too long ago I saw an article about the town he came from.
And I phoned the Italian Embassy, but they couldn't help me.
Then I phoned the attache, the commercial attache, and they gave me the-- the phone number and the address.
I wrote, but I never-- I wrote to him, whether it was him, whether it is him, but I never got an answer.
So maybe he-- maybe he didn't get the letter or maybe it wasn't him.
We stayed in Neugraben over the winter.
Why do I remember that?
Because it was Christmas Day.
It was a very clear day, and there was an air raid on Hamburg.
And you could see the-- the planes, and it was very vivid.
And it-- this stays in my mind.
The air raids, they're fantastic.
We loved the air raids.
Because the more they were bombing, the more we thought the-- the-- the war will end.
And they-- the-- the bombs came, the noise, woooo, one wave after another.
We didn't worry about the bombs.
We worried about the anti-aircraft.
You know, they're like splinters, metal splinters, and they killed-- when-- when they fall back on earth, they can kill you.
So when we were working in the sandpit, we would take those wagons, and we would turn them over and we would hide under them.
And you could hear the way the-- the little pieces of metal would go on.
Well, fortunately enough that nothing happened to us in Neugraben.
But I don't know why, they always tried to move us around.
And-- and after that, they moved us to-- to Tiefstack.
In Tiefstack I worked not far away from the camp in a factory where we made bricks.
I was making these square bricks with the holes in it and also the ones for the roof, the-- the-- the straight ones.
And in the beginning of February, we walked out from-- from the factory to the entrance to the camp, and the sirens went for the air raid.
And we had a German guard with us.
We called him Pappie.
He was nicely rotund.
He was-- he was a nice guy.
He never-- they had to wear a gas mask, but they never had a gas mask in it.
They had all kinds of other things.
They might have an apple in it, a little piece of bread or something.
So Pappie was very nice.
And it was, you see, when there's an-- an attack, there's-- they call it fore alarm.
That means before the attack.
That means when-- when the planes cross the Channel and came to the land, to Europe, to the mainland, then it became a fore alarm.
That means now they're really here.
And the second time, the sirens went.
And so we wanted to go in, and they said no, that we can't go in because they have to count us.
And next to me was a girl by the name of Suzie Glazer, and she was carrying a pail of water.
Because we changed every day, somebody else, so we could wash in warm water.
And as we were standing there waiting to be let in, the air raid started.
So the gates open, we started to run.
I ran through the dining hall out the back.
And we got-- they really let us have it but real good.
Pappie was killed.
Suzie Glazer was killed.
I don't know how many girls were killed.
There were some wounded.
The-- Dr.
Gold, she was our physician, she was wounded.
I landed in the back of the dining hall outside.
And somehow I got to myself, and I looked and I had the SS man under my arm.
We fell.
We were just at the right spot, because if I would have been further here, the bomb would have got me.
Or if I would have been further here, the pressure of the bomb would have torn my lungs.
So we were just here in the-- in the middle.
So the-- the camp was demolished, and we were put up in the factory for a few days till the Germans figured out what they're going to do with us.
And we there moved to Bergen-Belsen.
Of course, we didn't know where we were moving.
And as we were waiting for the train to be-- take us, we were-- we were sitting there on the-- on the grass-- an air raid came, but nothing happened.
And they loaded us up, and they took us who knows where.
And we arrived in the middle of the night, and we started walking, and we found out it was Bergen-Belsen.
An aside from that.
When I came to Canada, I met a man who worked for-- he was a-- a purchasing agent, I don't know, for Woolworth or Kresges.
And of course it was 1949.
It was short-- it wasn't that far off, not that long after the war.
So he asked me where I was, and I said I was in Hamburg, and he says he was in the Air Force.
And-- and I told them, yes, I said, the Air Force, they really fixed us.
They-- they bombed us.
He says, in Hamburg?
I said yes.
Was it near the chimneys?
I said yes.
He started to cry.
He said, it was my plane.
We went to the Dresden.
That was the time that they were bombing Dresden.
Our camp was near a power station.
That means the chimneys.
So it was-- at that time, they didn't have much of radar.
They-- they only had these-- these points kind of to relate to it.
And he said, yes, we had a misfortune.
We had to let the bombs go.
And that's what-- what happened.
His name, I think, is Butler.
I'm not quite sure, but I think his name was Butler.
It's a long time ago since 1949.
No, he was Canadian.
So we came to Bergen-Belsen.
There were no bunks.
There was nothing.
It was horror of horrors.
It was really the worst of the them.
There was no food.
There was no water.
There were no toilets.
There were only dead people.
[SIGHS] I never cried.
But when I had to go to the toilet, and I had to go real fast, and I had to stumble over-- over people lying on the floor.
And I was afraid that something was going to happen and, you know, that's when I cried.
There was a lady with me by the name of Maresova.
We called her [?
Baba ?] Maresova.
She was older than we were.
We-- we kind of smuggled her through.
She would clean up the mess around the block in the morning, and she could get a little more to eat, not that we had any, and she would share everything with me.
She was really great.
And so this was just-- people got sick and people had lice and people had typhoid and it was terrible.
The Germans ran away, because we didn't know that the front was coming closer.
And they ran away, and so Hungarian soldiers arrived.
Well, they weren't any better.
And I must tell you, they were shooting left and right.
The Germans had their turnips and potatoes, you know, like in storage.
They were like mounds.
So the girls would go and dig them up so to have something to eat, because nobody-- nobody gave us anything to eat.
And-- and these Hungarians just would go and just would shoot them because they were digging those rutabagas.
Anyway, nobody was there.
Everybody left us.
I don't know how it was that somebody said that the Red Cross is coming.
I-- I can't remember that.
So there were about four or five of us, said, we have to prepare for the Red Cross.
We took a table-- found a table somewhere.
We went walking with a table on the-- there was no-- no soldiers, no nothing.
Everybody left.
There were some SS women who were with us, and one of them was called Bubbie.
And Bubbie came and said she wants that we should hide her, because she knows something is going on.
And we said, of course, no way, Bubbie.
Bubbie, go where you came from.
That wasn't her real name.
We called her Bubbie.
And so we were walking with this table to the entrance of the camp, and there at once there was this huge tank with a white star on it.
And the turret opened, and a young man with curly hair appeared and he said, a division of the Allies is coming to liberate you.
And in the meantime, some Germans must have been hiding because they started shooting at us.
And the girl next to me, her name was [?
Herzog, ?] she had a-- a shoulder pad, and one of the bullets lodged in her shoulder pad.
And since this tank came and-- and the doors opened, we went to the side, and they-- the British started coming in.
They had no idea that we were there.
It just happened that they took that little road from the main road.
They came from Hanover.
You know, it was a fighting unit.
They came in, and there was this little road, so they started to investigate.
And here they found this camp, and here they found this horror.
They were stunned.
They didn't know what to do with us.
So I suppose they-- they-- they started talking to headquarters, what-- what they found.
And the first thing they did, they put up showers for us.
And so we could, the ones who were walking around-- there were very few of us-- could take showers.
They started taking people out, only the ones who were very ill.
The soldiers, they're all covered up, like, you know, like you saw them when they went to the Gulf War.
You know, they had all this-- everything covered up because they were afraid to catch typhoid from us, because there were so many lice and typhoid and things like that.
And you only could go out completely naked.
So they put a stretcher down, and before you knew, you had 10 naked women there.
But of course, they took it one after the other.
OK.
